title: Thai Green Curry with Homemade Paste

ingredients.name: boneless chicken thighs, cut into 3 cm pieces

subs.name: 400 g firm tofu or mixed vegetables (brinjal, zucchini, baby corn)
subs.notes: reduce simmering time to 8 minutes for tofu

ingredients.name: coconut milk, full-fat
visual_checks: 400 ml; do not shake — the thick cream on top is used first
ingredients.notes: 400 ml; do not shake — the thick cream on top is used first

ingredients.name: fresh green chillies (bird's eye or serrano)
lemongrass stalks, tender white part only
kaffir lime leaves
visual_checks: 4 go into the paste; 4 torn for cooking
ingredients.notes: 4 go into the paste; 4 torn for cooking

subs.name: 1 tsp finely grated lime or lemon zest
subs.notes: add at the same stage, a little goes a long way

ingredients.name: galangal or fresh ginger, thinly sliced
visual_checks: galangal is traditional; fresh ginger is a fine substitute
ingredients.notes: galangal is traditional; fresh ginger is a fine substitute

subs.name: 2 tbsp light soy sauce plus a pinch of salt
subs.notes: loses some depth but still tasty

subs.name: 1 tsp brown sugar or regular sugar

ingredients.name: Thai basil or holy basil leaves
visual_checks: fresh tulsi is botanically close and works well
ingredients.notes: fresh tulsi is botanically close and works well

steps.text: Blend the green chillies, lemongrass, 4 kaffir lime leaves, galangal (or ginger), garlic, cumin, coriander seeds and shrimp paste (if using) with 3 tablespoons of water until a smooth, fragrant paste forms. A blender gives the finest result; a food processor or mixie also works. Set aside.

Open the coconut milk without shaking it. Spoon the thick cream off the top into a cold wok or kadai — you should get about 5-6 tablespoons. Heat over medium-high flame until the cream bubbles and sizzles, about 2-3 minutes.

Add all the green curry paste to the bubbling coconut cream. Stir-fry continuously on medium-high heat for 3-4 minutes until the paste darkens slightly, turns fragrant and the oil begins to separate at the edges — this step is essential to remove the raw taste.

Add the chicken pieces and toss to coat in the paste. Stir-fry on high heat for 3 minutes until the outside of the chicken is sealed and coloured, though it will still be raw inside — that is expected at this stage.
Pour in the remaining coconut milk and 100 ml of water. Add the fish sauce, jaggery and the remaining 4 torn kaffir lime leaves. Stir everything together and bring to a gentle simmer.

Cover and simmer on low-medium heat for 15 minutes, stirring once or twice, until the chicken is fully cooked through and the sauce has thickened slightly. Taste — it should be creamy, mildly sweet and fragrant. Adjust fish sauce for saltiness and jaggery for sweetness.
Turn off the heat and stir in the Thai basil or tulsi leaves. The residual heat wilts the leaves and releases their aroma without destroying it. Serve immediately over steamed jasmine rice.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → 400 g firm tofu or mixed vegetables (brinjal, zucchini, baby corn) (reduce simmering time to 8 minutes for tofu); fish-sauce → 2 tbsp light soy sauce plus a pinch of salt (loses some depth but still tasty); jaggery → 1 tsp brown sugar or regular sugar; kaffir-lime-leaves → 1 tsp finely grated lime or lemon zest (add at the same stage, a little goes a long way)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A kadai works perfectly in place of a wok — high heat and a heavy base are all that matter. Galangal is sold as 'Thai adrak' in some metro specialty stores (Chennai, Mumbai, Bengaluru); fresh ginger (adrak) is an excellent everyday substitute. Holy basil (tulsi) plants are found in most Indian homes and work well in place of Thai basil. Full-fat coconut milk cans are widely available on BigBasket and Amazon India. If fish sauce is unavailable, use 2 tbsp soy sauce and a pinch of salt.
